_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and microbiology from Brook 2011 (PMID 21640048) and Lewis et al. 2010 BMJ. Mumps data from WHO 2024 surveillance. Sialendoscopy data from Marchal et al. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2007. Sjögren association from Vitali et al. ACR/EULAR 2016. Juvenile recurrent parotitis from Schneider et al. 2014. Acute suppurative parotitis from McAdams et al. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 1996.

heroOverview: Parotitis is inflammation of one or both parotid glands, the largest of the major salivary glands lying anterior to the ear. Causes range from viral infection (mumps remains a leading cause worldwide despite MMR vaccination), bacterial superinfection in dehydrated elderly or post-operative patients, salivary stones, autoimmune disease (Sjögren syndrome), HIV-associated lymphoepithelial cysts, granulomatous disease, drug effects, and recurrent juvenile parotitis. Mumps alone caused approximately 700,000 reported cases worldwide in 2019 (WHO surveillance), and acute bacterial parotitis affects up to 0.01-0.02% of post-operative adults. Patients typically present with painful pre-auricular swelling, tenderness, dry mouth, fever, and difficulty chewing. Untreated bacterial parotitis can progress to abscess, facial nerve injury, and septicaemia. Most cases resolve with hydration, sialogogues, gentle gland massage, targeted antibiotics, and treatment of underlying causes; sialendoscopy and surgical drainage are reserved for complicated cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Acute bacterial parotitis: approximately 0.01-0.02% of hospitalized adults; mumps parotitis: estimated 700,000 reported cases globally in 2019 (WHO); chronic parotitis affects approximately 1 in 5,000 adults
keyStats.demographics: Mumps peak: children and unvaccinated young adults; acute bacterial: postoperative or dehydrated elderly; juvenile recurrent: ages 3-15; chronic obstructive: middle-aged adults
keyStats.avgAge: Mumps parotitis: 5-15 years; bacterial parotitis: 50-80 years; sialolithiasis: 30-60 years; Sjögren-related: 40-60 years
keyStats.globalCases: Mumps causes the largest global burden of parotitis; sialolithiasis affects approximately 1.2% of population on autopsy series; HIV-associated parotitis affects 5-10% of HIV-positive adults

definition: Parotitis (ICD-10: K11.20-K11.23 for sialadenitis; B26 for mumps) is inflammation of one or both parotid glands. The parotid gland is the largest salivary gland, weighing 14-28 g, located anterior and inferior to the auricle and wrapped around the masseter muscle, with the parotid (Stensen's) duct emptying opposite the upper second molar. The gland is divided by the facial nerve into superficial and deep lobes. Parotitis can be acute (developing over hours to days) or chronic (recurrent or persistent over months). Causes are infectious (viral including mumps, parainfluenza, influenza, EBV, CMV, HIV, coxsackievirus; bacterial including Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, anaerobes; mycobacterial), obstructive (sialolithiasis, ductal stricture), inflammatory or autoimmune (Sjögren syndrome, IgG4-related disease, sarcoidosis), drug-induced (antihypertensives, anticholinergics, antidepressants, iodine), HIV-associated lymphoepithelial cysts, and rare (granulomatous, neoplastic mimics). Acute bacterial parotitis (also called acute suppurative parotitis) typically occurs in dehydrated, postoperative, or elderly patients where reduced salivary flow allows ascending oral bacteria; Staphylococcus aureus accounts for 50-90% of cases. Sialolithiasis (salivary stones) affects the submandibular gland (Wharton's duct) more often than parotid, but parotid stones produce recurrent meal-time swelling. Sjögren syndrome causes bilateral chronic parotitis with sicca symptoms. The condition is managed by otolaryngology, oral surgery, rheumatology, and infectious disease specialists depending on cause.

typesClassification.description: Most commonly mumps virus, but parainfluenza, influenza, EBV, CMV, HIV, coxsackievirus, and adenovirus can also cause acute parotitis. Mumps typically bilateral with malaise, low-grade fever, and 5-10 days of swelling. Complications include orchitis, meningitis, pancreatitis, and rarely deafness.

Caused by ascending oral bacteria, predominantly Staphylococcus aureus (50-90%), Streptococcus, and oral anaerobes. Occurs in dehydrated, postoperative, or elderly patients with reduced salivary flow. Risk of abscess formation and facial nerve dysfunction.

Recurrent episodes of unilateral or bilateral parotid swelling and pain in children aged 3-15, lasting 3-7 days, often without systemic features. Imaging shows sialectasis. Usually resolves with puberty but some progress to adult chronic parotitis.

Recurrent painful parotid swelling at mealtimes when saliva production rises; caused by stones (more common in submandibular gland but occurs in parotid) or strictures. Diagnosis confirmed by ultrasound, CT, sialography, or sialendoscopy.

Bilateral chronic parotid enlargement with dry mouth (xerostomia), dry eyes (keratoconjunctivitis sicca), and positive anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. Increased risk of lymphoma in long-standing disease.
Bilateral cystic enlargement of the parotid glands in HIV-positive patients; 5-10% prevalence. Painless and often diagnosed on routine examination. Antiretroviral therapy reduces gland size.
Mycobacterial (tuberculosis, atypical mycobacteria), sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease, and cat-scratch disease can produce chronic parotid enlargement with or without lymphadenopathy.

primarySymptoms: Painful swelling in front of and below the ear, sometimes filling the gap between the angle of the jaw and the mastoid.
Tenderness on palpation of the parotid gland, with the lobule of the ear lifted and pushed outward.
Pain that worsens with eating (especially sour foods or citrus) when obstructive causes drive saliva production.
Fever, malaise, and reduced oral intake, particularly in acute bacterial and mumps parotitis.
Difficulty chewing, opening the mouth (trismus), and turning the head because of pain.
Purulent discharge from the parotid duct (Stensen's opening, opposite the upper second molar) on gland massage in acute bacterial parotitis.
Dry mouth (xerostomia) and dry eyes (keratoconjunctivitis sicca) in Sjögren syndrome.
Bilateral painless enlargement in HIV-associated lymphoepithelial cysts.
Recurrent swelling at mealtimes that resolves between meals in obstructive parotitis.
Headache, vomiting, and neck stiffness if mumps progresses to meningoencephalitis (rare).
earlyWarningSigns: Tender swelling in front of the ear in a child, adolescent, or unvaccinated adult — exclude mumps
Recurrent painful parotid swelling at mealtimes — suspect sialolithiasis
Bilateral parotid enlargement with dry mouth and dry eyes — screen for Sjögren syndrome
Postoperative or dehydrated elderly patient with new parotid pain and swelling — exclude acute bacterial parotitis
Bilateral painless cystic parotid swelling — exclude HIV-associated lymphoepithelial cysts
Recurrent unilateral parotid swelling in a school-age child without systemic features — juvenile recurrent parotitis
emergencySigns: Severe trismus, dysphagia, drooling, or stridor — exclude parapharyngeal extension or airway compromise
Spreading neck cellulitis, induration, or crepitus — suspect deep-neck-space infection or necrotising fasciitis
Facial weakness or asymmetry suggesting facial nerve involvement — emergency review for abscess or malignancy
Sudden severe headache, neck stiffness, and altered consciousness — exclude mumps meningoencephalitis
Acute severe testicular pain and swelling in a male with mumps — mumps orchitis with risk of subfertility
Septic appearance with hypotension and tachycardia — suspect bacterial parotitis with sepsis

causes.description: Paramyxovirus transmitted by respiratory droplets; produces 7-10 days of bilateral parotid swelling, fever, and malaise after a 14-25 day incubation. Vaccination with MMR reduces incidence dramatically but outbreaks occur in unvaccinated or partially vaccinated populations, including university cohorts.

Ascending infection by oral bacteria, predominantly Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA), Streptococcus species, and anaerobes. Occurs when salivary flow is reduced by dehydration, postoperative state, debilitation, medication, or obstruction.

Calcium phosphate or carbonate stones form in the salivary duct, obstructing flow and producing recurrent painful swelling at mealtimes. Submandibular gland is more commonly affected (80-90% of stones); parotid stones account for 10-20%.

Chronic lymphocytic infiltration of salivary and lacrimal glands with anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. Causes bilateral parotid enlargement, dry mouth, and dry eyes; raises long-term lymphoma risk.

Bilateral cystic and solid enlargement of the parotid glands in 5-10% of HIV-positive adults. Often painless. Aetiology involves polyclonal lymphoid hyperplasia and cyst formation. Antiretroviral therapy reduces gland size.

Many drugs reduce salivary flow (anticholinergics, antihistamines, antidepressants, diuretics, iodinated contrast) and predispose to acute bacterial parotitis. Direct drug-induced gland enlargement also occurs with iodine and some antiretroviral therapies.

Recurrent episodes of parotid swelling and pain in children aged 3-15, of uncertain aetiology, possibly congenital ductal abnormalities and Mycobacterium-related triggers. Usually resolves with puberty.

Chronic granulomatous infection or inflammation can produce parotid enlargement. Tuberculosis, atypical mycobacteria, sarcoidosis (Heerfordt syndrome with uveitis, parotitis, and fever), and IgG4-related disease (Mikulicz syndrome) all enter the differential for persistent gland enlargement.

riskFactors.description: Mumps incidence rises in populations with low MMR vaccine coverage. Two-dose MMR vaccination provides 88% protection; outbreaks have occurred in vaccinated cohorts with waning immunity, prompting third-dose recommendations in some regions.
Reduced salivary flow allows ascending bacterial infection. Common in elderly hospitalized patients, postoperative recovery, and those on anticholinergic or diuretic therapy.

Stones in the salivary duct produce obstruction, stasis, and infection. Higher prevalence in patients with chronic dehydration, smoking, and certain metabolic conditions (hyperparathyroidism, hyperuricaemia).
Female-predominant autoimmune disease (9:1 ratio) producing chronic parotid enlargement. Often coexists with rheumatoid arthritis and lupus.
5-10% of HIV-positive adults develop bilateral parotid lymphoepithelial cysts; antiretroviral therapy reduces incidence and gland size.
Antidepressants, antihistamines, antihypertensives, anticholinergic agents, diuretics, and chemotherapy reduce salivary flow and raise parotitis risk.
Both raise the risk of bacterial parotitis by altering oral flora and reducing salivary clearance.

riskFactors.factor: Radiation to the head and neck
riskFactors.description: Radiotherapy for head and neck cancer can permanently reduce salivary flow and predispose to chronic and acute parotitis.

affectedDemographics: Children and unvaccinated young adults: mumps parotitis remains a global concern despite MMR vaccination.
Elderly hospitalized patients, particularly postoperative or dehydrated: acute bacterial parotitis.
Adults aged 40-60 with Sjögren syndrome (90% female): chronic bilateral parotitis with sicca symptoms.
HIV-positive adults: 5-10% develop bilateral lymphoepithelial cysts.
Children aged 3-15 with juvenile recurrent parotitis: recurrent episodes that typically resolve with puberty.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a focused history (acute vs chronic, unilateral vs bilateral, pain related to eating, fever, mumps contact, vaccination history, HIV status, current medications, recent surgery or dehydration, sicca symptoms) and examination (inspection of swelling pushing the lobule outward, palpation for tenderness and firmness, intraoral inspection for purulent discharge from Stensen's duct on gland massage, cervical lymphadenopathy, facial nerve function, eye examination, and complete oral cavity assessment). Blood tests include full blood count with differential (lymphocytic predominance in mumps; neutrophilia in bacterial), CRP, serum amylase (raised in mumps), HIV testing, mumps IgM and IgG serology, anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies, ANA, complement, rheumatoid factor, and quantitative immunoglobulins (raised IgG4 in IgG4-related disease). Saliva from Stensen's duct is sent for bacterial culture and Gram stain when bacterial infection is suspected. Imaging: ultrasound is first-line for parotid pathology, identifying glandular oedema, abscesses, ductal dilation, stones, and lymphoepithelial cysts. CT or MRI with sialography is used for complex or chronic disease, suspected deep-neck extension, or pre-operative planning. Sialendoscopy (a minimally invasive technique using a small endoscope through Stensen's duct) is both diagnostic and therapeutic for ductal stones and strictures. Fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) or core biopsy is used when neoplasm is suspected. Schirmer test, salivary flow measurement, and minor salivary gland biopsy support Sjögren diagnosis. Differential diagnosis includes sialolithiasis, sialadenitis of other glands, parotid tumours (pleomorphic adenoma, Warthin tumour, malignancy), Bartonella or atypical mycobacterial infection, Kawasaki disease, lymphoma, and chronic granulomatous disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination with bimanual palpation and intraoral inspection
diagnosticTests.purpose: Localizes swelling, identifies tenderness, detects pus from Stensen's duct, and excludes deep-neck extension
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 15-20 minute outpatient assessment. The examiner palpates externally and intraorally while milking the gland toward the duct opposite the upper second molar.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging; identifies abscess, stones, ductal dilation, lymphoepithelial cysts, and tumours
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless 15-20 minute clinic scan. Real-time imaging, often available the same day. Distinguishes diffuse inflammation from focal lesions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates complex chronic disease, ductal anatomy, deep-neck involvement, and pre-operative anatomy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-30 minute scan, sometimes with intraductal contrast. Used in selected cases when ultrasound is non-diagnostic or surgery is planned.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Minimally invasive endoscopy of the parotid duct; both diagnostic and therapeutic for stones and strictures
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-60 minute procedure under local or general anaesthesia. The endoscope is passed through Stensen's duct opening. Allows stone retrieval and balloon dilation of strictures.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies causative organism and informs antibiotic choice
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Saliva or pus from Stensen's duct is sent in aerobic and anaerobic transport. Results in 24-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Mumps IgM and IgG serology, paramyxovirus PCR
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for serology; oral swab for PCR. Results in 1-7 days. IgM positive in acute infection; IgG indicates previous exposure or vaccination.

diagnosticTests.test: Sjögren syndrome work-up (anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB, ANA, Schirmer test, minor salivary gland biopsy)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses Sjögren syndrome in patients with chronic bilateral parotitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for antibodies; Schirmer test in clinic with filter paper; minor salivary gland biopsy is a 30-minute outpatient procedure under local anaesthetic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes HIV-associated parotid lymphoepithelial cysts in patients with bilateral parotid swelling
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 1-3 days. Consent and counselling per local guidelines.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on the underlying cause and is individualized. Acute bacterial parotitis is treated with empirical antibiotics targeting Staphylococcus aureus and oral flora (intravenous flucloxacillin 1-2 g 6-hourly or co-amoxiclav 1.2 g 8-hourly, with vancomycin or clindamycin if MRSA is suspected), aggressive rehydration (oral or intravenous), warm compresses, gentle gland massage from the tail of the gland toward the duct, sialogogues such as lemon drops or sour sweets to stimulate salivary flow, and analgesia. Most patients improve within 48-72 hours; abscess formation (5-10%) requires drainage by needle aspiration, sialendoscopic intervention, or open incision with facial nerve protection. Mumps and other viral parotitis are managed with supportive care including hydration, analgesia, and bed rest; isolation for 5 days from onset of parotid swelling reduces transmission. Complications such as orchitis require analgesia and scrotal support, meningoencephalitis needs hospital admission and neurological monitoring. Recurrent juvenile parotitis is managed conservatively in most cases with hydration, gland massage, and sialogogues during episodes; sialendoscopy with ductal lavage is highly effective in reducing recurrence. Sialolithiasis is treated with sialendoscopic stone retrieval (first-line for ductal stones), extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy for selected stones, or open surgical removal for large parenchymal stones. Sjögren syndrome is managed by rheumatology with artificial saliva substitutes, pilocarpine 5 mg four times daily, cevimeline, hydroxychloroquine 200-400 mg daily, and rituximab in refractory disease; meticulous dental and ocular care is essential because of dry mouth and dry eyes. HIV-associated parotid lymphoepithelial cysts respond to antiretroviral therapy with gland size reduction; surgery is reserved for cosmetic or compressive concerns. IgG4-related disease and granulomatous parotitis are treated with corticosteroids and rituximab. Patient education includes hydration, oral hygiene, MMR vaccination (two doses), avoidance of anticholinergic medications when possible, and recognition of warning signs. Surgical parotidectomy is rare and reserved for chronic disease unresponsive to medical and minimally invasive treatment, with careful facial nerve preservation.

medicalTreatments.name: Empirical intravenous antibiotics for acute bacterial parotitis
medicalTreatments.description: Flucloxacillin 1-2 g IV 6-hourly or co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV 8-hourly; vancomycin or clindamycin if MRSA suspected; piperacillin-tazobactam for severe or immunocompromised cases. Switched to oral after improvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response within 48-72 hours in over 80% of properly hydrated patients

medicalTreatments.name: Aggressive hydration and sialogogues
medicalTreatments.description: Oral hydration (2-3 L/day) or intravenous fluids in dehydrated patients; sialogogues (lemon drops, sour sweets, citrus juice diluted) stimulate salivary flow and flush the gland.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined with antibiotics, accelerates resolution of bacterial parotitis and reduces recurrence

medicalTreatments.name: Warm compresses and gentle gland massage
medicalTreatments.description: External warm compress 4-6 times daily; gentle bimanual gland massage from the tail of the parotid toward Stensen's duct opening encourages drainage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunct measures supporting medical therapy; safe and patient-administered

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesia (paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly plus ibuprofen 400 mg 8-hourly)
medicalTreatments.description: Combined first-line analgesia controls pain. Short courses of stronger analgesia for severe cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate pain control in 80-90% of patients without opioids

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive care for viral parotitis (mumps)
medicalTreatments.description: Hydration, analgesia, bed rest, isolation for 5 days from parotid swelling. Treat complications (orchitis, meningoencephalitis) as they arise.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Most patients recover fully within 7-10 days; complications occur in 5-15%

medicalTreatments.name: Pilocarpine 5 mg orally 4 times daily or cevimeline for Sjögren-related xerostomia
medicalTreatments.description: Muscarinic agonists that stimulate salivary flow in Sjögren syndrome. Common side effects include sweating and gastrointestinal upset.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest symptomatic improvement in 50-60% of patients with retained gland function

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine 200-400 mg daily for Sjögren syndrome
medicalTreatments.description: Disease-modifying agent that may reduce inflammation and improve sicca and joint symptoms. Annual eye examination is required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic benefit in 40-60% of patients; modest reduction in glandular inflammation

medicalTreatments.name: Antiretroviral therapy for HIV-associated parotid disease
medicalTreatments.description: Combination antiretroviral therapy reduces parotid gland size and lymphoepithelial cysts within months of starting effective treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant reduction in gland enlargement and cyst size in 70-80% of patients on effective ART

surgicalOptions.name: Sialendoscopy with stone retrieval and ductal lavage
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive endoscopy through Stensen's duct under local or general anaesthesia; stones up to 4-7 mm can be retrieved with baskets, larger stones fragmented with laser. Ductal strictures treated with balloon dilation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone clearance in 80-90% of accessible parotid stones; symptomatic improvement in 70-90% of juvenile recurrent parotitis after lavage

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage of parotid abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Performed under general anaesthesia with careful facial nerve identification and protection; a small incision over the most prominent point of the abscess allows drainage. Drain placed for 24-72 hours.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution in 90-95% with appropriate antibiotic cover; risk of transient facial nerve dysfunction 5-10%

surgicalOptions.name: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) for selected stones
surgicalOptions.description: Non-invasive fragmentation of salivary stones using focused acoustic waves. Used for stones not accessible to sialendoscopy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone fragmentation in 50-80%; complete clearance in 30-60% depending on stone size and location

surgicalOptions.name: Superficial or total parotidectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for chronic refractory parotitis, recurrent disease unresponsive to all medical and minimally invasive measures, or coexisting tumour. Performed with facial nerve dissection and preservation under general anaesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure of refractory disease in 90-95%; permanent facial nerve injury under 5% in expert hands; Frey syndrome (gustatory sweating) in 30-50%

surgicalOptions.name: Open transcutaneous stone removal
surgicalOptions.description: For large parenchymal stones not amenable to sialendoscopy or ESWL. Skin incision with careful facial nerve preservation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone removal in 95%; modest risk of facial nerve injury and salivary fistula

alternativeTreatments.name: Saliva substitutes and oral lubricants
alternativeTreatments.description: Artificial saliva products (sprays, gels, lozenges) provide symptomatic relief for xerostomia in Sjögren syndrome and post-radiation patients. Used alongside hydration and pilocarpine.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and acupressure
alternativeTreatments.description: Limited evidence for symptomatic relief in chronic salivary gland disorders, particularly Sjögren-related xerostomia. May be a useful adjunct in selected patients.

whySeeSpecialist: An otolaryngologist or oral and maxillofacial surgeon assesses the gland, performs sialendoscopy, drains abscesses, and plans definitive treatment. Rheumatology is involved for Sjögren syndrome, infectious disease for HIV-related parotitis and chronic granulomatous disease, and paediatric ENT for juvenile recurrent parotitis. Urgent referral is needed for facial nerve weakness, abscess, or systemic illness.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a centre with parotid surgical experience, sialendoscopy capability, ultrasound on site, and links to rheumatology, infectious disease, and oral medicine. Ask about facial nerve outcomes for parotid surgery and success rates of sialendoscopy in chronic and recurrent disease.
hospitalCriteria: Otolaryngology unit with parotid surgical capability
Ultrasound with experienced head and neck radiology
Multidisciplinary salivary gland clinic for chronic disease
keyFacilities: Tertiary ENT and salivary gland clinics
Paediatric ENT for juvenile recurrent parotitis
preventionStrategies: Ensure two-dose MMR vaccination for all children at ages 12-15 months and 4-6 years; consider third-dose booster during mumps outbreaks.
Maintain adequate hydration, especially in hospitalized, elderly, and postoperative patients.
Practice meticulous oral hygiene with twice-daily brushing, flossing, and 6-monthly dental review.
Stop smoking and limit alcohol to reduce sialolith and infection risk.
Review medications periodically and rationalize anticholinergic, antihistamine, and antidepressant drugs that reduce salivary flow.
Recognize early symptoms of parotitis and seek prompt assessment in postoperative or vulnerable patients.
Test for HIV in patients with unexplained bilateral parotid enlargement and offer antiretroviral therapy if positive.
Screen for Sjögren syndrome in patients with chronic bilateral parotitis and sicca symptoms.
lifestyleModifications: Drink 2-3 L of water daily to maintain salivary flow.
Suck on sugar-free lemon drops or chew sugar-free gum after meals to stimulate saliva.
Apply warm compresses to the affected area 4-6 times daily during acute episodes.
Massage the gland gently toward the duct outlet to encourage drainage.
Maintain meticulous oral hygiene and use fluoride toothpaste twice daily.
Avoid known triggers (citrus, spicy foods) only if they reproducibly worsen pain.
Quit smoking permanently and limit alcohol intake.
Stay up to date with vaccinations including MMR.

recommended: Adequate hydration (2-3 L of water daily)
Sour foods (lemon, citrus) to stimulate salivary flow during recovery
Soft cool foods (yoghurt, smoothies, ice cream) during severe acute episodes
Mediterranean-style diet rich in antioxidants for overall immune health
avoid: Dehydrating beverages (excess caffeine, alcohol) during acute illness
Very hot or spicy foods that aggravate pharyngeal pain
Sugary foods that promote dental caries in patients with xerostomia

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during acute illness; resume normal activity once afebrile and symptoms improve. Avoid contact sports until pain and swelling have settled. Patients with chronic disease can maintain regular aerobic and resistance exercise.
dailyManagement: Drink water regularly throughout the day.
Suck sugar-free sour sweets or chew sugar-free gum after meals.
Apply warm compresses for 10-15 minutes 4-6 times daily during episodes.
Perform gentle external gland massage from the gland tail toward the duct.
Take prescribed antibiotics or anti-inflammatories as scheduled.
Track symptoms (pain, swelling, fever) in a diary during chronic disease.
prognosis: Outcomes are generally favourable. Acute bacterial parotitis resolves within 7-10 days with antibiotics and hydration; abscess formation occurs in 5-10% but is treatable. Mortality from acute bacterial parotitis is under 1% in healthy adults but rises in debilitated, postoperative, or septic patients to 20-50%. Mumps parotitis is self-limiting in 1-2 weeks; complications (orchitis 15-30% in postpubertal males, meningoencephalitis 1-10%, deafness 1 in 20,000) reduce overall favourability but most patients recover fully. Juvenile recurrent parotitis usually resolves by puberty; sialendoscopy substantially reduces recurrence frequency. Sialolithiasis is curable with sialendoscopic stone retrieval in over 80% of accessible cases. Sjögren syndrome is chronic with a 15-20 fold elevated lymphoma risk and requires lifelong management; sicca symptoms substantially affect quality of life. HIV-associated parotid disease responds well to antiretroviral therapy. Quality of life is most affected by chronic disease, frequent recurrence, and complications from surgery. Recurrence prevention depends on hydration, oral hygiene, treatment of underlying autoimmune or systemic disease, and appropriate vaccination.
recoveryTimeline: Acute bacterial parotitis: symptomatic improvement within 48-72 hours; complete resolution in 7-10 days. Mumps parotitis: 7-10 days. Sialolithiasis after sialendoscopy: resolution within 1-2 weeks. Juvenile recurrent parotitis: episodes last 3-7 days with full recovery; condition usually resolves by puberty. Chronic Sjögren syndrome: lifelong management with intermittent flares.
complications: Parotid abscess formation (5-10% of bacterial cases) — requires drainage with facial nerve protection.
Facial nerve dysfunction (transient or permanent) from abscess pressure, surgical injury, or malignancy infiltration.
Deep-neck-space extension and parapharyngeal abscess — emergency drainage and broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Mumps complications: orchitis (15-30% of postpubertal males), meningoencephalitis (1-10%), oophoritis, pancreatitis, sensorineural deafness (1 in 20,000), and rarely subfertility.
Lymphoma in long-standing Sjögren syndrome (15-20 fold increased risk).
Frey syndrome (gustatory sweating) after parotid surgery — affects 30-50% of postoperative patients.
Salivary fistula after open surgery — usually resolves spontaneously or with pressure dressings.
Recurrent or chronic parotitis from inadequate treatment or persisting obstruction.

supportResources.name: AAO-HNS Patient Information — Salivary Gland Disorders
supportResources.description: Patient resources from the American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery on salivary gland disorders including parotitis.

supportResources.name: NHS — Mumps
supportResources.description: Plain-language patient guide to mumps and parotitis in the UK NHS.

supportResources.name: Sjögren's Foundation
supportResources.description: Leading patient charity for Sjögren syndrome providing education, advocacy, and support.

supportResources.name: CDC — Mumps
supportResources.description: Public-health information on mumps, MMR vaccination, and outbreak management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mumps (with or without orchitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mumps is a specific viral cause of parotitis; diagnosis is confirmed by IgM serology or PCR. Bacterial parotitis features purulent discharge from Stensen's duct, neutrophilia, and responds to antibiotics. Mumps complications (orchitis, meningoencephalitis) need specific attention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sjögren Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sjögren syndrome causes chronic bilateral painless parotid enlargement with dry mouth, dry eyes, and positive anti-Ro/SSA antibodies. Acute parotitis is painful, often unilateral, with systemic features and a specific infective or obstructive cause.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ludwig Angina
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ludwig angina is bilateral submandibular cellulitis with floor-of-mouth swelling and tongue elevation. Parotitis localizes to the pre-auricular area and lifts the ear lobule. Both can compromise the airway but originate from different fascial spaces.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peritonsillar abscess presents with severe one-sided sore throat, trismus, drooling, and a bulging soft palate displacing the uvula. Parotitis produces pre-auricular swelling lifting the ear lobule and tenderness over the parotid gland.

relatedConditions.name: Sjögren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common autoimmune cause of chronic bilateral parotitis; sicca symptoms and antibody testing distinguish it from infective parotitis.

relatedConditions.name: Mumps
relatedConditions.relevance: Major viral cause of acute parotitis worldwide; prevented by two-dose MMR vaccination.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Bilateral parotid lymphoepithelial cysts affect 5-10% of HIV-positive adults; antiretroviral therapy reduces gland size.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Can coexist with secondary Sjögren syndrome and parotid enlargement; rheumatological assessment guides treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Ludwig Angina
relatedConditions.relevance: Another deep-neck-space infection that can mimic or coexist with severe parotitis; distinguished by location and submandibular involvement.

faqs.question: What is parotitis?
faqs.answer: Parotitis is inflammation of one or both parotid glands, the largest salivary glands lying in front of the ear. Causes include viral infection (especially mumps), bacterial infection, salivary stones, autoimmune disease (Sjögren syndrome), HIV-associated cysts, and medications that reduce salivary flow.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of parotitis?
faqs.answer: Typical symptoms are painful swelling in front of and below the ear that lifts the ear lobule outward, tenderness on palpation, pain worsened by eating, fever, malaise, and difficulty chewing. Bacterial parotitis may produce pus from Stensen's duct in the mouth. Mumps usually presents bilaterally.

faqs.question: How is parotitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, supported by ultrasound of the parotid gland, bacterial culture of duct discharge, mumps serology or PCR, Sjögren-related antibodies, HIV testing where indicated, and sialendoscopy in chronic cases. CT or MRI is used for complex disease or pre-operative planning.

faqs.question: How is parotitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on cause: hydration, sialogogues, warm compresses, and gland massage for all; intravenous antibiotics (flucloxacillin or co-amoxiclav) for bacterial; supportive care and isolation for mumps; sialendoscopic stone removal for sialolithiasis; pilocarpine and immunomodulation for Sjögren; antiretroviral therapy for HIV-associated disease.

faqs.question: Is parotitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Viral parotitis (especially mumps) is highly contagious through respiratory droplets; patients should be isolated for 5 days from the onset of parotid swelling. Bacterial parotitis is not contagious. Sjögren-related and HIV-associated forms are not transmissible through casual contact.

faqs.question: Can mumps cause parotitis even after vaccination?
faqs.answer: Yes. Two-dose MMR vaccination provides approximately 88% protection. Outbreaks have been reported in fully vaccinated cohorts, particularly university students, where waning immunity contributes. Symptoms in vaccinated patients tend to be milder and shorter.

faqs.question: What is sialolithiasis?
faqs.answer: Sialolithiasis is the formation of stones in salivary gland ducts. Submandibular stones are most common (80-90%); parotid stones (10-20%) produce recurrent painful swelling at mealtimes. Sialendoscopy retrieves stones up to 4-7 mm; larger stones may need lithotripsy or open surgery.

faqs.question: What is sialendoscopy?
faqs.answer: Sialendoscopy is a minimally invasive procedure using a small endoscope (0.8-1.6 mm) inserted through the salivary duct (Stensen's or Wharton's) to visualize the duct, remove stones, dilate strictures, and irrigate the gland. Performed under local or general anaesthesia as a day case.

faqs.question: Can children get parotitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mumps is a major cause in unvaccinated children. Juvenile recurrent parotitis affects children aged 3-15 with recurrent episodes of unilateral or bilateral swelling lasting 3-7 days. Most cases resolve by puberty; sialendoscopy with ductal lavage substantially reduces recurrence.

faqs.question: What is Sjögren syndrome?
faqs.answer: Sjögren syndrome is an autoimmune disease producing chronic bilateral parotid and lacrimal gland inflammation with dry mouth, dry eyes, and positive anti-Ro/SSA antibodies. It affects mostly women aged 40-60 and is managed with saliva substitutes, pilocarpine, hydroxychloroquine, and rituximab in refractory cases.

faqs.question: Why is hydration important in parotitis?
faqs.answer: Adequate hydration maintains salivary flow, which flushes the gland and reduces bacterial ascent through the duct. Dehydration is a major precipitant of acute bacterial parotitis in elderly and postoperative patients. Aim for 2-3 L of fluid per day during recovery.

faqs.question: Can parotitis cause facial nerve weakness?
faqs.answer: The facial nerve runs through the parotid gland and can be compressed by abscesses, infiltrated by tumours, or injured during surgery. Acute uncomplicated parotitis rarely causes facial weakness; new facial nerve dysfunction needs urgent investigation for abscess or malignancy.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for parotid swelling?
faqs.answer: Seek prompt review for any new persistent parotid swelling, especially with pain, fever, or facial weakness. Urgent assessment is needed for severe trismus, drooling, spreading neck swelling, facial nerve dysfunction, signs of sepsis, or testicular pain in males with mumps.

faqs.question: What antibiotics treat bacterial parotitis?
faqs.answer: First-line is intravenous flucloxacillin or co-amoxiclav targeting Staphylococcus aureus and oral anaerobes. Clindamycin or vancomycin is used if MRSA is suspected. Piperacillin-tazobactam is reserved for severe or immunocompromised cases. Oral antibiotics follow once the patient is afebrile and tolerating intake.

faqs.question: Can parotitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence is common in juvenile recurrent parotitis, chronic Sjögren syndrome, and obstructive disease from stones or strictures. Hydration, oral hygiene, treatment of underlying autoimmune disease, and sialendoscopy reduce recurrence frequency. Some patients need parotidectomy for refractory disease.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for parotitis?
faqs.answer: Surgery is rarely needed. Sialendoscopy treats most ductal stones and strictures. Open drainage is needed for abscesses (5-10% of bacterial cases). Parotidectomy is reserved for chronic refractory disease and tumours, with careful facial nerve preservation in expert hands.

faqs.question: What is Frey syndrome?
faqs.answer: Frey syndrome is gustatory sweating that occurs in 30-50% of patients after parotid surgery. Aberrant nerve regeneration causes sweating and flushing over the parotid area during eating. Topical antiperspirants, botulinum toxin injection, and rarely surgical correction help symptomatic cases.

faqs.question: Is parotid swelling always serious?
faqs.answer: Most acute parotid swelling is benign and self-limiting, especially viral parotitis. However, persistent unilateral swelling, facial nerve weakness, hard fixed masses, or rapid growth raise concern for malignancy and require urgent imaging and FNAC biopsy.

faqs.question: How long does parotitis last?
faqs.answer: Acute bacterial parotitis resolves within 7-10 days with appropriate antibiotics and hydration. Mumps parotitis lasts 7-10 days. Juvenile recurrent parotitis episodes last 3-7 days. Chronic Sjögren syndrome is lifelong but managed with intermittent flares. HIV-associated parotid disease responds to antiretroviral therapy over months.

faqs.question: Can adults get mumps parotitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Although mumps is traditionally considered a childhood illness, outbreaks among university students and young adults are increasingly common, partly because of waning vaccine immunity. Adult mumps carries higher rates of orchitis (15-30% of postpubertal males) and meningoencephalitis than childhood disease.

faqs.question: How can I prevent parotitis?
faqs.answer: Two-dose MMR vaccination prevents most mumps. Maintain adequate hydration, good oral hygiene, and regular dental care. Review medications that reduce saliva flow. Treat dental and gingival infections promptly. Stop smoking and limit alcohol. Manage underlying conditions such as Sjögren syndrome and HIV.
